title_en: Later Companions and Martyrs of the Early Battles
content_en:
The companion generation was not a uniform cohort of people who all came to Islam at the same moment and shared a single common experience. Usd al-Ghabah's biographical entries reveal a generation internally differentiated by the timing and circumstances of each person's acceptance of Islam, and by the nature of the contribution each person made to the early Muslim community. Ibn al-Athir is attentive to these distinctions, and his work provides an essential guide to the internal stratification of the companion generation as the classical scholars understood it.

The earliest Muslims — those who accepted Islam in Mecca before the Hijra to Medina — occupy the highest rank in the scholarly tradition's assessment. Among these the very first group (the Sabiqun al-Awwalun — the early forerunners) is distinguished again: Khadijah, Abu Bakr, Ali ibn Abi Talib, Zayd ibn Harithah, and the earliest circle who came through Abu Bakr's personal missionary activity in the first days. The Companions who accepted Islam after the Hijra but before the pivotal events like Badr — and especially those who participated in Badr itself — form another distinguished rank. The 'People of Badr' (Ahl Badr) constitute one of the most honored sub-categories in the entire companion canon: the scholarly tradition records that the Prophet said of them that Allah had looked upon the people of Badr and said 'Do what you wish, for I have forgiven you.' This statement — preserved in the Sahih collections — made the Badr Companions uniquely important in Islamic jurisprudence: their later actions were sometimes used as legal evidence precisely because of the assurance of divine acceptance associated with their status.

The martyrs of the early battles form a distinct and important category that Usd al-Ghabah documents with particular care. Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib — the Prophet's uncle and one of the most formidable warriors of early Islam — was killed at Uhud and his body mutilated by Hind bint Utbah, who had lost her own family members at Badr. Hamzah's entry in the biographical dictionaries emphasizes both his physical prowess and his spiritual rank: the Prophet called him Sayyid al-Shuhada — Master of the Martyrs — and reportedly wept over his mutilated body. His grave at Uhud remains one of the most visited sites in the Islamic world.

Mus'ab ibn Umayr was among the most remarkable of the early martyrs. From a wealthy Meccan family, he was the Prophet's first ambassador — sent to Medina before the Hijra to teach the people of Medina Islam and Quran. His persuasive teaching was responsible for a substantial portion of the early Medinan acceptance of Islam. He was killed at Uhud, and the Prophet's grief over him is recorded in the sources: when they came to wrap him in a shroud, the shroud was so short that it could cover his head but not his feet, or his feet but not his head. The Prophet instructed that his head be covered and his feet wrapped with grass, and he recited the verse: 'Among the believers are men who have been true to what they pledged to Allah' (33:23). Ibn al-Athir's entry for Mus'ab captures both the early Muslim experience of the sudden reversal of worldly fortune — from wealth to poverty for the sake of faith — and the dignity with which the early Companions accepted this reversal.

The late Companions — those who accepted Islam in the period between Hudaybiyyah (6 AH) and the Prophet's death (11 AH) — include some of the most historically important figures in early Islamic history. Khalid ibn al-Walid accepted Islam in the year before the Conquest of Mecca, having previously been one of the Quraysh's most effective military opponents. After his conversion, the Prophet gave him the title Sayf Allah — Sword of Allah — and he became one of the most consequential military commanders in the rapid expansion of the early Islamic state. His entry in Usd al-Ghabah navigates the complexity of his biography: his extraordinary military contributions, his later command under Abu Bakr in the Riddah wars and the Syrian campaigns, and the controversial incident at Khaybar and later at Banu Jadhimah where his actions generated criticism from other Companions and from the Prophet himself.

Amr ibn al-As, who accepted Islam in the same period as Khalid, is another figure whose biography in Usd al-Ghabah must navigate considerable complexity. His diplomatic skills, his early governance of Oman, his role in the conquest of Egypt, and his later involvement in the political conflicts after the Prophet's death make him one of the most consequential and most contested figures in early Islamic history. Ibn al-Athir records the biographical essentials without suppressing the controversies, applying the standard Sunni scholarly approach: charity toward Companions in matters where scholars have debated their motivations, and reliance on established historical accounts rather than polemical reconstructions.

Among the categories that Usd al-Ghabah documents with care is the group known as the Muallafah Qulubuhum — those whose hearts were reconciled, meaning those who received from the Prophet financial gifts or other considerations to encourage and consolidate their acceptance of Islam. These include some prominent Meccans who converted at the Conquest and were given generous portions of the spoils of Hunayn. Ibn al-Athir records these distributions and the companions who received them without the apologetic awkwardness that sometimes characterizes popular treatments of these accounts. The practice of winning hearts through generosity was considered by the Prophet and the early Muslim scholars a legitimate instrument of building the Muslim community, and the companions who benefited from it — many of whom became genuinely committed Muslims, including Abu Sufyan's family — were recorded as full members of the companion generation.

The picture of the later Companions in Usd al-Ghabah is thus one of extraordinary diversity: warriors and scholars, merchants and farmers, former slaves and tribal chiefs, reluctant converts who became pillars of the faith and enthusiastic early believers who struggled with later trials. The companion generation's internal variety is itself one of the strongest arguments for Islam's universal appeal, and it is preserved in biographical literature precisely so that subsequent generations can know the full breadth of who answered the Prophet's call.
